MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT ; Or, In The Alternative, Mtn For Summ Adjudication (Ntc)
Off Calendar Apr-07-2014 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES - ATTY. DAN CROWLEY & ATTY. SCOTT P. BRUST, TEL. NO. (707) 525-8999 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT;
NO OTHER APPEARANCES ON THE MATTER.

RULING - The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT HOUSE OF AIR'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT; Or, In The Alternative, Motion For Summary Adjudication. Granted as to House of Air's Motion for Summary judgment. The defendant has demonstrated a complete defense exists as to all causes of action. The signed waiver of liability (Crowley Decl., Ex. C.) is a complete defense as to the second, third, fourth, and fifth causes of action. The signed release is clear, unambiguous, and explicit in releasing all claims and causes of action against the Defendant, including any claims arising from Defendant's own negligence. The evidence shows that Plaintiff understood the language and effect of the release. Thus, summary judgment is granted as to Plaintiff's second cause of action for products negligence, third cause of action for breach of express warranty, fourth cause of action for breach of implied warranty, and fifth cause of action for ordinary negligence. While the release is ineffective to bar Plaintiff's first cause of action for strict product liability, Defendant has adequately demonstrated that Plaintiff cannot establish an element, where a condition precedent to maintaining a strict liability claim is that the primary objective was to acquire ownership or use of a product, and not to obtain a service. (See Westlye v. Look Sports, (1993) 17 Cal.App.4th 1715, 1747; Ferrari v. Grand Canyon Dories (1995) 32 Cal.App.4th 248, 258.) The evidence shows that Defendant provides additional services, over and above the mere use of trampoline equipment (see Ontiveros v. 24 Hour Fitness Corp, (2008) 169 Cal.App.4th 424, 434.) Thus, summary judgment is proper as to Plaintiff's first cause of action for strict liability. While the release is ineffective to a claim of gross negligence, Defendant has demonstrated a complete defense in the assumption of risk doctrine. Defendant's arrangement of trampolines cannot be said to be "totally outside the range of ordinary activity" (see Freeman v. Hale, (1994) 30 Cal. App. 4th 1388, 1394), in light of the inherent risks of the sport of trampolines. The burden then shifted to Plaintiff to show "a want of even scant care" or "an extreme departure from ordinary conduct" (Wallace v. Busch Entm't Corp., 837 F. Supp. 2d 1093, 1101 (S.D. Cal. 2011).). The evidence does not show that Defendant's conduct rose to the level of "scant care" or an "extreme departure from ordinary conduct." Thus, summary judgment as to Plaintiff's sixth cause of action for gross negligence is granted. Plaintiff concedes that no evidence of misrepresentation has been discovered. (P's Opp, 2: 15-16.) Defendant's motion for summary judgment as to the seventh cause of action for intentional misrepresentation, and the eighth cause of action for negligent misrepresentation, is granted. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith, Clerk: Cynthia Herbert ; Court Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR # 5976 =302/ehg
=(302/EHG)
